label: Ad platform
placeholder: Select the advertising platform
helper: Choose where the ads will be published so the correct asset structure and length rules can be applied.
why: The platform determines the required ad assets, formatting conventions, CTAs, and character handling.

label: Ad format
placeholder: Choose a format or use Auto
helper: Auto selects a suitable native format based on the platform and campaign goal.
why: Different formats require different copy elements, such as hooks, cards, headlines, or responsive assets.

label: Product or offer
placeholder: Describe what you are advertising and what the customer receives.
helper: Include the product, service, event, application, subscription, or lead offer being promoted.
why: The model cannot accurately describe or position an unspecified product or offer.

label: Campaign goal
placeholder: Select the main campaign goal
helper: Choose the single most important action or outcome for this campaign.
why: The campaign goal determines the message hierarchy, conversion friction, and CTA.

label: Target audience
placeholder: Who should see this ad, and what makes the offer relevant to them?
helper: Describe the audience using useful business or interest context without including private personal data.
why: Audience relevance materially changes the language, angle, benefits, and level of explanation.

label: Core value proposition
placeholder: What is the clearest reason someone should consider this offer?
helper: Focus on the main value rather than a long list of features.
why: The central value proposition provides the primary persuasive idea for the campaign.

label: Offer details
placeholder: Add verified prices, discounts, dates, bonuses, trial terms, or availability.
helper: Only include terms that are current, accurate, and approved for advertising.
why: Real offer terms can materially strengthen conversion copy but must not be invented.

label: Destination URL
placeholder: https://example.com/landing-page
helper: Enter the page users should reach after clicking the ad.
why: The destination helps identify the intended action and alerts the output to landing-page consistency requirements.

label: Output language
placeholder: English
helper: Specify the language in which all generated copy should be written.
why: Language cannot always be inferred and can affect character use, tone, and platform limits.

label: Testing angles
helper: Choose between 1 and 5 meaningfully different ad angles.
why: Paid advertising benefits from distinct testable concepts rather than one isolated piece of copy.

label: Audience awareness
placeholder: Select how familiar the audience is
helper: This controls how much context, education, and direct selling the ad should use.
why: Cold and warm audiences require materially different openings and conversion approaches.

label: Problem or need
placeholder: What situation, frustration, task, or need makes the offer relevant?
helper: Describe the need without using shame, fear, or unsupported assumptions about individuals.
why: A supplied need helps make the ad specific without forcing the model to invent audience pain points.

label: Desired outcome
placeholder: What practical result is the audience hoping to achieve?
helper: Use a realistic outcome that the product can genuinely support.
why: The desired outcome helps translate features into relevant benefits.

label: Key differentiators
placeholder: What makes this offer meaningfully different from common alternatives?
helper: Include only distinctions that can be explained and substantiated.
why: Verified differentiation supports stronger positioning and prevents generic advertising.

label: Audience objections
placeholder: What concerns might stop someone from clicking, signing up, or buying?
helper: Examples may include price, complexity, trust, setup time, fit, or switching effort.
why: Real objections can produce useful test angles and more credible copy.

label: Approved proof points
placeholder: Add verified results, specifications, certifications, ratings, or evidence.
helper: Only provide claims that may be used publicly and can be substantiated.
why: The model needs approved evidence before it can safely create proof-led advertising.

label: Target market
placeholder: For example: United States, Germany, EU, or worldwide
helper: Add the country, region, or jurisdiction when it affects wording, currency, eligibility, or disclosures.
why: Advertising rules, offer availability, and market expectations can differ by jurisdiction.

label: Brand name
placeholder: Enter the public brand or business name
helper: Use the name that should appear in the ad or advertising account.
why: Some platforms require a business name, while other ads may need clear advertiser identification.

label: Brand voice
placeholder: Select the closest brand voice
helper: Choose the overall communication style rather than a temporary campaign emotion.
why: Brand voice changes wording and presentation while preserving the same factual message.

label: Tone modifiers
placeholder: Add optional tone qualities
helper: Select only qualities that should noticeably influence this campaign.
why: Tone modifiers allow campaign-level adjustment without duplicating the brand-voice field.

label: Creative context
placeholder: Describe the image, video, product shot, demonstration, or existing creative.
helper: The generated copy should complement the creative rather than contradict or repeat it.
why: Effective paid-ad copy must align with what the audience will actually see.

label: Search keywords
placeholder: List priority searches or keyword themes, separated by commas.
helper: Use verified keyword targets from the relevant search campaign.
why: Search-ad headlines and descriptions should reflect the intent of the supplied keyword set.

label: Video opening
placeholder: What happens or appears during the first few seconds?
helper: Describe the opening shot, action, speaker, demonstration, or available footage.
why: A video hook should match the real opening visual rather than describe footage that does not exist.

label: Carousel cards
helper: Choose between 2 and 10 cards.
why: The number of cards directly determines the message progression and amount of card copy.

label: Preferred CTA
placeholder: Select a CTA or use Auto
helper: Auto chooses a standard platform CTA that matches the campaign goal.
why: The preferred action affects both button selection and the closing language of the ad.

label: Preferred ad angles
placeholder: Select angles or let the model choose
helper: Only select angles that match the available facts and campaign strategy.
why: Advertisers may need specific strategic angles while the model still prevents unsupported use of proof or offers.

label: Regulated category
placeholder: Select a category when relevant
helper: This activates more cautious claim handling and additional review notes.
why: High-stakes and regulated advertising requires materially different wording and review safeguards.

label: Required disclosures
placeholder: Paste approved legal text, eligibility terms, warnings, or required qualifiers.
helper: Use exact approved wording where possible and state where it must appear.
why: The model cannot safely invent legally required disclosures or jurisdiction-specific wording.

label: Claims to avoid
placeholder: List prohibited promises, phrases, comparisons, or product claims.
helper: Include internal brand restrictions and wording that must not appear.
why: Advertiser-specific restrictions cannot always be inferred from general policy rules.

label: Additional requirements
placeholder: Add any remaining instructions that materially affect the final ad package.
helper: Use this for essential requirements not covered by the other fields.
why: One controlled catch-all field prevents multiple overlapping miscellaneous inputs.
